title: Commonwealth English

infobox.value: is the language as practised in the Commonwealth; the term is most often interchangeable with *[British English](/wiki/British_English "British English")*, but is also used to distinguish between British English and that in the rest of the Commonwealth.[[3]](#cite_note-3) English in the Commonwealth is diverse, and many regions have developed their own local varieties of the language. The [official status](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of English varies; in [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladeshi_English "Bangladeshi English"), it lacks any but is widely used, and likewise in [Cyprus](/wiki/Languages_of_Cyprus "Languages of Cyprus"), it is not official but is used as the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")*.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Ammon_et_al._2006-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Commonwealth English](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_English&redirect=no "Commonwealth English"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Please_Do_Not_Step_or_Sit_on_Graves_-_Genocide_Memorial_Center_-_Kigali_-_Rwanda.jpg/250px-Please_Do_Not_Step_or_Sit_on_Graves_-_Genocide_Memorial_Center_-_Kigali_-_Rwanda.jpg)](/wiki/File:Please_Do_Not_Step_or_Sit_on_Graves_-_Genocide_Memorial_Center_-_Kigali_-_Rwanda.jpg)

[English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Kinyarwanda](/wiki/Kinyarwanda "Kinyarwanda") text in Kigali, [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"). Rwanda, a Commonwealth country, was not previously part of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").

The use of the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") in current and former [countries](/wiki/Member_states_of_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations "Member states of the Commonwealth of Nations") of [the Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") was largely inherited from [British colonisation](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), with some exceptions. English forms part of the Commonwealth's [common culture](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations#Culture "Commonwealth of Nations") and serves as the medium of inter-Commonwealth relations.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

**Commonwealth English** is the language as practised in the Commonwealth; the term is most often interchangeable with *[British English](/wiki/British_English "British English")*, but is also used to distinguish between British English and that in the rest of the Commonwealth.[[3]](#cite_note-3) English in the Commonwealth is diverse, and many regions have developed their own local varieties of the language. The [official status](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of English varies; in [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladeshi_English "Bangladeshi English"), it lacks any but is widely used, and likewise in [Cyprus](/wiki/Languages_of_Cyprus "Languages of Cyprus"), it is not official but is used as the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")*.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Ammon_et_al._2006-5)

Written English in current and former Commonwealth countries generally favours British English spelling as opposed to that of [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) with some exceptions, particularly in Canada, where there are strong influences from neighbouring American English.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Native varieties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Native varieties")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Abenaki_stop_sign_odanak.jpg/250px-Abenaki_stop_sign_odanak.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abenaki_stop_sign_odanak.jpg)

Multilingual stop sign in a [First Nations reserve](/wiki/Indian_reserve "Indian reserve") in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), featuring the [Abenaki](/wiki/Abenaki_language "Abenaki language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") and English languages

[Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") native varieties of English began to develop during the 18th century, with the colonisation of Australasia and South Africa. [Australian English](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English") and [New Zealand English](/wiki/New_Zealand_English "New Zealand English") are closely related to each other and share some similarities with [South African English](/wiki/South_African_English "South African English"). Nonetheless, South African English has unique influences from indigenous African languages, and [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") influences inherited alongside the evolution of [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans"), while New Zealand English has a lot of influences from the [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language") language.[[8]](#cite_note-Bayard2000-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[Canadian English](/wiki/Canadian_English "Canadian English") contains elements of [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English") and [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English"), as well as many [Canadianisms](/wiki/A_Dictionary_of_Canadianisms_on_Historical_Principles "A Dictionary of Canadianisms on Historical Principles") and some [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") influences. It is the product of several waves of immigration and settlement, from Britain, Ireland, France, the United States, and around the world, over a period of more than two centuries.[[10]](#cite_note-Dollinger_2008-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Mother_and_daughter_in_India_1920.jpg/250px-Mother_and_daughter_in_India_1920.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mother_and_daughter_in_India_1920.jpg)

The mother tongue of [Anglo-Indians](/wiki/Anglo-Indian_people "Anglo-Indian people") is English, whilst most Indians speak it as a second language.

In many Commonwealth countries, there exists a relatively small native Anglophone minority amongst a larger population who speak English as a second language; [Anglo-Indians](/wiki/Anglo-Indian_people "Anglo-Indian people") speak English as their mother tongue, but it is not the first language of most Indians.[[12]](#cite_note-Andrews2013-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Africa")]

See also: [British diaspora in Africa](/wiki/British_diaspora_in_Africa "British diaspora in Africa") and [Commonwealth diaspora](/wiki/Commonwealth_diaspora "Commonwealth diaspora")

In addition to South Africa, a number of Commonwealth countries in Africa have native varieties of English. A community of native English speakers exists in [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwean_English "Zimbabwean English"); the country's dialect bears features of British English, South African English and other Southern Hemisphere varieties of Commonwealth English.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Also in [Southern Africa](/wiki/Southern_Africa "Southern Africa") and with historical influence from South Africa, [Namibia](/wiki/Namlish "Namlish") and [Botswana](/wiki/Botswana "Botswana") have their own dialects,[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) with smaller native English-speaking populations.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) The same is true of [Kenya](/wiki/Kenyan_English "Kenyan English")[[20]](#cite_note-:0-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) and [Uganda](/wiki/Ugandan_English "Ugandan English") in [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa").[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Caribbean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Caribbean")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/No_Politics_nor_Religion_in_Bar_sign%2C_Placencia_Belize_2021.jpg/250px-No_Politics_nor_Religion_in_Bar_sign%2C_Placencia_Belize_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:No_Politics_nor_Religion_in_Bar_sign,_Placencia_Belize_2021.jpg)

Sign in [Belizean Creole](/wiki/Belizean_Creole "Belizean Creole"), an English-based creole language

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Mackennal_%281926%29_Shakespeare_Memorial.jpg/250px-Mackennal_%281926%29_Shakespeare_Memorial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mackennal_(1926)_Shakespeare_Memorial.jpg)

[William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") memorial in Sydney, [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"). English is part of the common culture of the Commonwealth.

[Caribbean English](/wiki/Caribbean_English "Caribbean English") is drawn from British English and West African languages. It is influenced by constant contact with English-based [Creoles](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language"). There is considerable influence from [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") and other South Asian languages in countries with language Indian populations, including [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"), and [Guyana](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana"). [Jamaican English](/wiki/Jamaican_English "Jamaican English") and [Barbadian English](/wiki/Bajan_English "Bajan English") bear influences of [Irish English](/wiki/Hiberno-English "Hiberno-English").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Non-native varieties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Non-native varieties")]

Second-language varieties of English in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") and [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") have often undergone "[indigenisation](/wiki/Indigenization "Indigenization")"; that is, each English-speaking community has developed (or is in the process of developing) its own standards of usage, often under the influence of local languages. These dialects are sometimes referred to as *New Englishes*;[[25]](#cite_note-25) most of them inherited [non-rhoticity](/wiki/Rhoticity_in_English "Rhoticity in English") from Southern British English.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Africa")]

See also: [West African Pidgin English](/wiki/West_African_Pidgin_English "West African Pidgin English")

In the Commonwealth countries of [West Africa](/wiki/List_of_English_dialects "List of English dialects"), there are national varieties of English in [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigerian_English "Nigerian English"),[[29]](#cite_note-29) [Ghana](/wiki/Ghanaian_English "Ghanaian English"),[[30]](#cite_note-:02-30) [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leonean_English "Sierra Leonean English"),[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroonian_English "Cameroonian English")[[32]](#cite_note-Ngefac-2025-32) and [the Gambia](/wiki/Gambian_English "Gambian English").[[33]](#cite_note-WolfPeter2008-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In East Africa, national varieties of English are spoken in Kenya, [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwandan_English "Rwandan English"), Uganda, and [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzanian_English "Tanzanian English").[[20]](#cite_note-:0-20)[[35]](#cite_note-WEGATE-35)[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22)[[36]](#cite_note-36) The modern development of English in Rwanda is closely associated with the post-1994 period. The return of Rwandans who had lived for extended periods in neighbouring countries, particularly Uganda, increased the presence of English in public and institutional life, and provided an important regional input for local English usage.[[37]](#cite_note-WEGATE2-37)[[38]](#cite_note-Samuelson2010-38) English subsequently expanded in domains such as education and administration.[[37]](#cite_note-WEGATE2-37)[[39]](#cite_note-Spowage2025-39) Rwanda became a Commonwealth country in 2009.[[40]](#cite_note-NYT_admission-40)

Prior to [Togo](/wiki/Togo "Togo")'s admission to the Commonwealth in 2022, foreign minister [Robert Dussey](/wiki/Robert_Dussey "Robert Dussey") said that he expected Commonwealth membership to provide opportunities for Togolese citizens to learn English.[[41]](#cite_note-:2-41)

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Asia")]

#### Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

[Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") ceased to be part of the Commonwealth by virtue of being a British territory in 1997. Nonetheless, [the English language there](/wiki/Hong_Kong_English "Hong Kong English") still enjoys official status.[[42]](#cite_note-Eoyang-42)

#### Indian subcontinent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Indian subcontinent")]

See also: [Englishisation § South Asia](/wiki/Englishisation#South_Asia "Englishisation")

English was introduced to [the subcontinent](/wiki/The_subcontinent "The subcontinent") by the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"). [India](/wiki/India "India") has the largest English-speaking population in the Commonwealth, although comparatively very few speakers of [Indian English](/wiki/Indian_English "Indian English") are first-language speakers.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) The same is true of English spoken in other parts of [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), including [Maldivian English](/wiki/Maldivian_English "Maldivian English"),[[45]](#cite_note-45) [Pakistani English](/wiki/Pakistani_English "Pakistani English"),[[46]](#cite_note-46) [Sri Lankan English](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_English "Sri Lankan English"),[[47]](#cite_note-47) [Bangladeshi English](/wiki/Bangladeshi_English "Bangladeshi English")[[48]](#cite_note-48) and [Myanmar English](/wiki/Myanmar_English "Myanmar English"); though [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") or Burma is not a Commonwealth country, English is the mother tongue of the [Anglo-Burmese](/wiki/Anglo-Burmese_people "Anglo-Burmese people") population.[[49]](#cite_note-49) [South Asian English](/wiki/South_Asian_English "South Asian English") is fairly homogeneous across the subcontinent, though there are some differences based on various regional factors.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

#### Malay Archipelago

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Malay Archipelago")]

[Southeast Asian English](/wiki/Southeast_Asian_English "Southeast Asian English") includes [Singapore English](/wiki/Singapore_English "Singapore English"), [Malaysian English](/wiki/Malaysian_English "Malaysian English"), and [Brunei English](/wiki/Brunei_English "Brunei English") as well as other varieties in non-Commonwealth countries (such as Indonesian English); it is not only the result of British colonisation but also American colonisation (as in the case of [the Philippines](/wiki/Philippine_English "Philippine English")) and globalisation. It has interacted with diverse local ecologies, shaping its form, function and status in the region.[[51]](#cite_note-51)
